segments.inferno-24-g60: Leva’mi is an archaic contracted form where the verb levò (passato remoto of levare) appears with the enclitic mi attached: literally “raised me.” The subject is omitted (third person, Virgil). Allor is an adverb (“then”) placed after the verb in typical poetic word order.
segments.inferno-24-g61: Mostrandomi is an older gerund/participle form (mostrando) with the enclitic mi “to/for me,” meaning “showing/making to appear to me.” Fornito is a past participle used predicatively with that participle to mean “appearing furnished/equipped.” Meglio di lena is a comparative phrase “better in (one’s) breath/strength,” and ch’ introduces a subordinate clause (che + i’ = che io) with sentia in the imperfect “I did not feel/notice.” Note the imperfect non mi sentia = “I did not feel it (in myself).”
segments.inferno-24-g62: E is the coordinating conjunction “and.” Dissi is passato remoto, first person singular of dire, meaning “I said,” and introduces the direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-24-g63: Va is the imperative singular of andare used as an interjection/command: “Go!” It begins the direct address inside quotation marks.
segments.inferno-24-g64: Ch’ is the clitic form of che introducing a content clause (“that”). I’ is the archaic subject pronoun/io (“I”). Son is the present tense of essere used here for the first person singular (“I am”) in the poetic form son (instead of sono). Forte and ardito are predicate adjectives joined by e (“and”).
segments.inferno-24-g65: Su per lo scoglio is a prepositional phrase “up over the rock/crag” (su + per). Prendemmo is passato remoto, first person plural of prendere, here used with la via to mean “we took the road/way.” The subject noi is omitted, as common in Italian.
segments.inferno-24-g66: Ch’ is the relative che (“which/that”) referring back to la via. Era is the imperfect of essere (“was”), and ronchioso is an adjective meaning “rugged” or “brambly,” used predicatively.
segments.inferno-24-g67: Stretto e malagevole are two predicate adjectives (“narrow and difficult/inconvenient”) coordinating with e. Their position after the verb is typical for descriptive predicates.
segments.inferno-24-g68: Ed is a form of e used before a vowel. Erto is an archaic adjective meaning “steep” (here predicative); the provided sense list for erto in the data is incorrect, so the specific sense index is unavailable. Più assai is a comparative with an intensifier (“much more/steeper by far”), che functions as “than” in comparative constructions, and quel di pria means “that (one) of before/earlier,” i.e. “than that of before.”
segments.inferno-24-g69: Parlando is a gerund/participial form 'speaking'. Andava (imperfect of andare) here conveys continuous or habitual action 'was going on / kept on' and combines with the gerund to mean 'was speaking'. Per non parer = 'in order not to appear' (per = 'in order to'); fievole = 'feeble/weak.' The whole phrase means 'he kept speaking so as not to seem weak.'
segments.inferno-24-g70: Onde here means 'whereupon' / 'from which' in the narrative sense ('and from that came...'). Una voce uscì is simple past 'a voice came out/issued'. De l’altro fosso = 'from the other ditch' (de = from/of; fosso = ditch/pit).
segments.inferno-24-g71: A parole formar is an infinitive construction meaning literally 'to form into words' (a + parole + formar). Disconvenevole is an adjective meaning 'unseemly/inappropriate' and modifies the implied words — 'forming unseemly words' or 'uttering words that were unseemly.'
segments.inferno-24-g72: Non so che disse is made of a present-tense main clause plus an indirect content clause: non + so (present indicative of sapere) = 'I do not know'; che introduces an indirect interrogative meaning 'what'; disse is the passato remoto (third person singular) of dire, 'he said.' The whole gives 'I do not know what he said.'
segments.inferno-24-g73: Ancor che begins a concessive subordinate clause meaning 'although' and normally takes the subjunctive; sovra 'l dosso is a prepositional phrase 'over/on the ridge' (sovra + the article l' + dosso). Fossi here functions as the (imperfect) subjunctive/verb form 'I was' (anchored to the concessive construction), de l'arco = 'of the arch', già = 'already', and che varca quivi is a relative clause ('which/that spans/crosses here') with varca in the present indicative.
segments.inferno-24-g74: Ma = 'but'; chi is a relative/pronominal subject 'who' ('the one who was speaking'); parlava is the imperfect 'was speaking/talking' (ongoing past action). Ad + infinitive (ad ire) indicates movement or tendency 'to go/leave' (here 'about to go' or 'to depart'); parea is the imperfect of parere 'seemed/appeared'; mosso is a past participle used as predicate adjective 'moved' (physically or emotionally) describing that subject.
segments.inferno-24-g31: Non era via is an existential/impersonal expression: 'there was no way' or 'no route existed.' The phrase da vestito di cappa qualifies that way as suitable 'for being dressed in a cloak' (da = 'for' / 'suitable for'), with vestito used adjectivally/participial ('dressed') and di cappa identifying the garment.
segments.inferno-24-g32: ché is a causal conjunction meaning 'for'/'because.' noi a pena is elliptical: a pena is an idiom meaning 'hardly' or 'with difficulty,' so the clause begins to say 'for we could hardly...' (the verb is supplied in the next segment).
segments.inferno-24-g33: ei is the archaic form for 'he' (egli); lieve is an adjective used adverbially ('light' / 'lightly') describing him; e io sospinto links the speaker as the second participant, with sospinto a past participle used adjectivally ('(I) pushed' or 'propelled'). The line describes the two of them: he was light, and I was the one pushed/propelling.
segments.inferno-24-g34: potavam is the imperfect indicative of potere, 'we could' / 'we were able' (here with the sense 'could manage'); sù + montar = 'to climb up' (sù indicates upward direction before the infinitive). di chiappa in chiappa is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'from buttock to buttock' (di = 'from' indicating source/starting point; in = 'into/on/onto' marking the successive buttock), describing how they climbed stepwise.
segments.inferno-24-g35: E se non fosse che introduces a counterfactual/conditional ('And if it were not that...') with fosse the imperfect subjunctive of essere. da quel precinto più che da l'altro specifies source/side ('from that rim more than from the other') explaining why ascent was easier; era la costa corta states the result 'the slope was short' (era = imperfect indicative 'was').
segments.inferno-24-g36: non so di lui is literal 'I do not know about him' — non negates, so is first-person present of sapere used to mean 'I don't know' or 'I can't speak for him,' and di lui marks the topic 'concerning him.'
segments.inferno-24-g37: ma introduces contrast ('but'); io sarei is the conditional of essere ('I would be'); ben is an intensifier here ('indeed, fully'); vinto is the past participle used adjectivally: 'overcome/defeated.' The whole means 'but I would certainly have been overcome.'
segments.inferno-24-g38: Ma is the simple adversative conjunction 'but'. Perché here introduces a cause ('because') rather than a question. Malebolge is the proper place-name; inver' is an archaic contracted preposition meaning 'toward' or 'facing' (literally 'in verso'), so inver' la porta means 'facing the gate'. Del bassissimo pozzo is a noun phrase 'of the very-low pit' (del = di + il). Tutta pende is a present indicative construction meaning 'it all leans/hangs' — tutta (entirely/the whole) modifies the subject (Malebolge understood) and pende (3rd sing.) is the verb 'hang/lean'.
segments.inferno-24-g39: Lo sito di ciascuna valle is 'the position/site of each valley' (lo sito = the site; ciascuna valle = each valley). Porta is a verb (3rd sing. of portare) meaning 'brings/causes' here, so the clause means 'the site causes that...'. Che introduces the subordinate clause 'that one side rises and the other descends'. L’una/l’altra are elliptical opposites ('the one' / 'the other'); surge and scende are present indicatives 'rises' and 'descends'.
segments.inferno-24-g40: Noi pur venimmo is 'we nevertheless came' (venimmo = passato remoto 'we came/reached'; pur = 'still/nevertheless'). Al fine 'to the end/at last' and in su la punta = 'up on the point/tip'. Onde here is used in the locative/relative sense 'from which' or 'whence' (introducing the place), l'ultima pietra si scoscende uses the reflexive si with scoscende (present) to mean 'the last stone drops/lets itself down' or 'is dislodged and falls down' — si makes the action intransitive/passive-like.
segments.inferno-24-g41: La lena = 'the breath' (lena = breath/strength). M'era munta del polmon is a figurative perfect construction: m'era munta (literally 'had been milked to me') = 'had been drained from my lungs' (era + past participle munta gives a state). Quand' io fui sù 'when I was up' places the time ('when I had come up').
segments.inferno-24-g42: Ch' is the conjunction che (= 'that' / 'so that') introducing the result clause. I' (io) is the subject 'I'; potea is the imperfect of potere 'could' and plus oltre = 'no longer/further' (più oltre = 'any further'), so the clause means 'that I could go no further.'
segments.inferno-24-g43: Anzi here works as 'instead' or 'rather' and introduces the corrective action. M'assisi is the past form of 'mi assi' (mi + assisi) meaning 'I sat myself down' (assisi = 'I sat' in the remote/poetic form). Ne la prima giunta = 'in/on the first junction/landing' (giunta = junction/landing of stairs).
segments.inferno-24-g133: E ïo al duca: is an elliptical speech-introduction. E = 'and'; ïo (archaic spelling with diaeresis marking stress) = 'I'; al duca = 'to the duke/guide'. The verb of saying is omitted here (common in Dante) and the phrase functions like 'and I (said) to the guide:'.
segments.inferno-24-g134: «Dilli che non mucci, is an imperative request. Dilli is an archaic contracted imperative equivalent to modern dìgli ('tell him'); che introduces the subordinate command; non mucci is a negative present form meaning 'do not be silent' (here subordinate to the imperative and using an older ending -i). Mucci is from muccio 'be silent'.
segments.inferno-24-g135: e domanda che colpa qua giù ’l pinse; continues the direct speech with another command. domanda is an imperative 'ask' (tu understood); che colpa = 'what fault' (che = 'what'); qua giù = 'here below'; ’l pinse is passato remoto 3rd-person singular of pingere used figuratively ('painted/marked' or 'struck' him) with clitic l' = 'him' placed before the verb. The whole clause means 'and ask what fault here below has marked/struck him.'
segments.inferno-24-g136: ch’io ’l vidi uomo di sangue e di crucci». is an explanatory subordinate clause introduced by ch' (that/for). io is emphatic 'I'; ’l is the object clitic 'him' attached before vidi (passato remoto 'I saw'); uomo di sangue e di crucci is a noun phrase 'a man of blood and of crucci' (i.e. violent/bloodthirsty and full of anger/wrath), with the repeated di giving poetic balance.
segments.inferno-24-g137: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to the prior narrative. ’l is the contracted article (il) attached by apostrophe, and peccator is the masculine noun (Dantean form of modern peccatore) serving as the sentence subject; the comma marks the relative clause that immediately follows.
segments.inferno-24-g138: che is a relative pronoun 'who/which' referring back to 'peccator'; ’ntese is the archaic past (passato remoto) form of intendere/entendere meaning 'heard' (Dante often drops the initial i). Together they mean 'who heard'.
segments.inferno-24-g139: non negates the clause. s’ is the reflexive clitic (si) attached to the verb, and infinse is an older/poetic perfect-form meaning 'feigned' or 'dissembled' (here in the passato remoto, 3rd singular): 'did not feign/pretend himself'.
segments.inferno-24-g140: ma 'but' contrasts the previous negation. drizzò is passato remoto 3rd singular of drizzare meaning 'directed/turned' (here 'turned toward'); verso + me is the prepositional phrase 'toward me'. l’animo e ’l volto are the two direct objects ('his mind/spirit and his face'), with the article l’ contracted before both nouns.
segments.inferno-24-g141: e 'and' links the participial description. di trista vergogna is an ablative/partitive phrase 'with sorrowful shame'; si is the reflexive clitic attached to dipinse, and dipinse is passato remoto of dipingere used figuratively: 'he painted/depicted himself with (an expression of) shame'.
segments.inferno-24-g142: poi is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards' and disse is passato remoto 3rd singular of dire: 'he said'. This introduces direct speech that follows.
segments.inferno-24-g143: Più mi duol is an idiom 'it grieves me more' (Più + verb). che here introduces the clause that causes the grief ('that/that you have...'). tu is the subject 'you'; m’ + hai + colto form a present perfect (hai colto) 'you have caught/found me' with the clitic m’ (me). ne la miseria = 'in the misery'; dove tu mi vedi is a relative clause 'where you see me'.
segments.inferno-24-g144: The second che is comparative 'than'. quando is 'when'; fui is passato remoto 1st singular of essere used as auxiliary ('I was') combined with the past participle tolto ('taken away'), so 'when I was taken from the other life' (de l’altra vita = 'from the other life').
segments.inferno-24-g145: Io 'I' is emphatic subject. non posso negar is the present tense 'I cannot deny' (posso + infinitive negar). quel che tu chiedi is a fused demonstrative + relative 'that which you ask' (quel + che introducing the subordinate clause).
segments.inferno-24-g146: in giù son messo is a stative perfect-like expression 'I am put down below' (son + past participle messo). tanto 'so much/so far' gives extent; perch’ (perché) introduces the causal clause 'because I was' (io fui, passato remoto). ladro a la sagrestia d’i belli arredi = 'a thief in the sacristy of the fine furnishings' (a = at/in; d’i = di + i = of the).
segments.inferno-24-g147: e 'and' links the clause. falsamente già fu apposto is a passive/perfect expression: già 'already', fu (passato remoto 3rd sing of essere) + apposto (past participle) = 'it had been already ascribed/attributed', and altrui means 'to another' (i.e. someone else was blamed).
segments.inferno-24-g148: Ma is a simple adversative conjunction meaning 'but.' Perché here functions in a final/purpose sense ('so that' / 'lest') rather than interrogative, introducing the idea the speaker wants to prevent: 'that you do not enjoy...'. Di tal vista is a prepositional phrase 'of such a sight' (di + tal[tale] + vista), with tal 'such' before the noun (archaic/poetic order is normal). Tu non godi is 2nd-person singular present (tu + non + godi); godere means 'to enjoy' and the present indicative is used idiomatically in this construction to express the outcome the speaker wishes to avoid.
segments.inferno-24-g149: Se introduces a conditional 'if' clause; mai is an adverb 'ever' (lit. 'if ever'); sarai is the future indicative of essere ('you will be'). Di fuor da is an older locative phrasing meaning 'out of' / 'outside of' (di + fuor + da), with fuor = 'out/outside' and da = 'from/of' (here part of the idiom 'di fuor da'); luoghi bui is 'dark places', with the adjective bui ('dark') following the noun as is common in poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-24-g150: Apri is the 2nd-person-singular imperative of aprire: 'open.' Li orecchi is the direct object 'the ears' using the archaic/dialectal plural article li (older form instead of modern gli); orecchi 'ears' is plural and receives the command. Al mio annunzio is a prepositional phrase 'to my announcement/message' (al = a + il), specifying where to direct the ears/listening.
segments.inferno-24-g151: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking the two imperatives. Odi here functions as a second-person-singular imperative ('hear' / 'listen'); its form coincides with a present form in some verbs, but context (paired with apri) shows it is a command addressed to 'tu.'
segments.inferno-24-g152: This is a compact clause with the place-name as subject (Pistoia) followed by the adverbial phrase 'in pria' ('first, formerly/before') and the prepositional phrase 'd’i Neri' ('of the Neri', i.e. belonging to the Neri faction). The verb 'si dimagra' is reflexive; here the reflexive 'si' gives an intransitive middle meaning ('becomes thin' / 'wastes away'). Word order (subject + adverbials + verb) is normal for poetic Italian.
segments.inferno-24-g153: 'Poi' is a temporal adverb ('then' / 'afterwards') introducing the next clause. 'Fiorenza' is the subject, 'rinova' is present indicative (he/she/it renews) used transitively, and 'gente e modi' are its direct objects = 'people and manners/customs'. The simple S V O order expresses a new consequence following the first clause.
segments.inferno-24-g154: 'Tragge' is an archaic/poetic 3rd-person singular present meaning 'draws' (from trarre); 'Marte' is the subject (Mars, here war). 'vapor di Val di Magra' is a partitive/genitive phrase 'vapour from/of the Val di Magra' (the first 'di' links vapor→Val, the second 'di' links Val→Magra). The relative clause begins with 'ch’è' ('which is') followed by 'di torbidi nuvoli involuto' — a predicate with 'involuto' (past participle used adjectivally, 'wrapped/enshrouded') and the adjective-noun 'torbidi nuvoli' ('turbid/murky clouds').
segments.inferno-24-g155: This clause begins with 'e' ('and') linking to the previous image. 'Con tempesta impetüosa e agra' is an instrumental/adverbial phrase ('with an impetuous and bitter tempest'). 'Sovra Campo Picen' is a locative phrase ('over/on the Picene Field/Plain'), and 'fia combattuto' is a future passive construction: 'fia' (poetic/fossil form of future auxiliary 'will be') + past participle 'combattuto' = 'will be fought' (i.e. there will be fighting there).
segments.inferno-24-g156: 'Ond’' (short for 'onde' / 'ondè') is a connective adverb meaning 'from which' / 'whence', linking the previous battle to its effect. 'Ei' is an archaic subject pronoun ('he/it') referring back to Mars (or to the motion/force described). 'Repente' ('suddenly') is an adverb modifying the verb 'spezzerà', which is future indicative 'he/it will break' (from 'spezzare'). The direct object 'la nebbia' ('the fog') completes the verb.
segments.inferno-24-g157: 'Sì ch’' is a fused construction meaning 'so that' or 'with the result that'; 'ogne' is an archaic form of 'ogni' = 'every'. 'Bianco' here is a substantive use of the adjective 'white' (the White faction/person), and 'ne' is a clitic pronoun referring back to the fog/vapour ('of it / by it'). 'Sarà feruto' is future passive ('will be struck/wounded'), so the whole means 'so that every White will be struck by it.'
segments.inferno-24-g158: This is a single clause: 'E' ('and') connects to what precedes. 'Detto l’ho' is a typical Italian clitic word order where the past participle 'detto' appears before the auxiliary clitic combination 'l’ho' (lo + ho) — literally 'it I have said' = 'I have said it'. 'Perché' here introduces a final/purposive clause ('so that'), not a question. 'Doler' is the infinitive 'to grieve / to cause pain' and 'ti debbia' uses the present subjunctive of 'dovere' (debbia) with the clitic 'ti' ('to you'): a purpose construction meaning 'so that it may/might grieve you.' The subjunctive expresses the wished-for/resultative consequence.
segments.inferno-24-g44: Omai is an adverb meaning 'by now' introducing the statement; convien is an impersonal present (from convenire) meaning 'it is necessary' or 'one must' and takes a subordinate clause introduced by che; that subordinate clause has the subject tu and the reflexive verb ti spoltre. Spoltre is an archaic/poetic reflexive verb (here in the dependent clause and understood as present-subjunctive mood because convien + che typically governs subjunctive) meaning to become slothful/idle, so the clause means 'that you thus make yourself slothful' or more idiomatically 'that you cast off/abandon such sloth'.
segments.inferno-24-g45: disse is passato remoto (past historic) of dire, 'said'; ’l is the contracted article il (the) before maestro; maestro is the noun 'master' or teacher — the whole phrase is simply 'the master said.'
segments.inferno-24-g46: ché here functions as a conjunction 'for' / 'because', introducing the reason that follows; it links the reported speech to the explanatory clause that follows.
segments.inferno-24-g47: seggendo is a present participle/gerund of the archaic verb seggere (to sit) meaning 'sitting'; in piuma is a locative phrase literally 'on down/feather' (i.e. sitting on comfortable bedding) and expresses the idea of being settled in ease or luxury.
segments.inferno-24-g48: in fama non si vien is an impersonal/passive construction: in fama = 'into/to fame'; non si vien = 'one does not come' or 'fame is not acquired' (si used impersonally to mean 'one' / 'people' / passive). Vien is present indicative of venire used in this impersonal sense.
segments.inferno-24-g49: né introduces a negative continuation 'nor'; sotto coltre is an idiomatic locative ('under a coverlet' or hidden under a quilt), and here the verb of the previous clause is understood (i.e. one does not come to fame sitting on down, nor [does one come to fame] under a coverlet).
segments.inferno-24-g50: sanza = 'without'; la qual is a feminine relative pronoun ('which') that refers back to fama; chi sua vita consuma is a generic/indefinite clause with chi 'whoever' as subject and consuma (present indicative of consumare) meaning 'spends' or 'wastes' his life — so 'without which (fame) whoever spends his life...' (the relative clause continues in the next line).
segments.inferno-24-g51: cotal is a contracted/poetic form of cotale 'such'; vestigio is 'trace' or 'footprint'; in terra di sé lascia follows an object–verb order where in terra ('on earth') and di sé ('of himself') specify where and whose trace it is, and lascia (present indicative of lasciare) means 'leaves' — 'leaves such a trace of himself on earth.'
segments.inferno-24-g52: qual introduces a comparative 'as' or 'such as'; fummo is an archaic/past form (passato remoto) of essere (here 'we were'); in aere e in acqua la schiuma is the noun phrase 'the foam in the air and in the water' — the line compares the transient trace people leave on earth to the ephemeral foam we were in air and water.
segments.inferno-24-g53: E 'and' connects to the previous speech; però is an adverb 'therefore/then'; leva is the second-person singular imperative of levare (here 'get up' or 'rise'), and sù is a particle/adverb meaning 'up'. The short clause functions as an exhortation: 'And therefore, rise up.'
segments.inferno-24-g54: vinci is the second-person singular imperative of vincere ('conquer' or 'overcome'), commanding the addressee to defeat l’ambascia ('the anguish/distress'); con l’animo is an instrumental phrase 'with the spirit/courage' and che vince ogne battaglia is a relative clause modifying animo ('that conquers every battle'), with vince in the present indicative meaning 'overcomes'. The clause thus reads 'Conquer the distress with the spirit that overcomes every battle.'
segments.inferno-24-g55: se is a conditional conjunction 'if' (introducing a proviso); col is the contraction of con + il 'with the'; suo modifies corpo ('his/its heavy body'); grave means 'heavy/weighty'; corpo is the noun 'body'; non negates; s’ (si) is the reflexive particle and here makes accascia reflexive ('collapse/sink down'); accascia is present indicative 'collapses' (so the condition reads 'if his heavy body does not collapse').
segments.inferno-24-g56: Più lunga scala literally 'a longer ladder/staircase' (più modifies lunga); convien again is the impersonal 'it is necessary'; che introduces the subordinate clause; si is the impersonal/reflexive particle used with sagliare, and saglia is the present subjunctive (or present of the archaic sagliare) in the clause required by convien: 'that one climb' — 'it is necessary that one climb a longer ladder.'
segments.inferno-24-g57: non basta is the negative construction 'it is not enough'; da costoro = 'from/by these (people)' (costoro = 'these people'/'those people'); esser is the infinitive 'to be' and partito is a past participle used substantively/adverbially 'departed' or 'separated' — the clause means 'it is not enough simply to have been separated from these people.'
segments.inferno-24-g58: Se is the conditional 'if' introducing the protasis; tu is the subject 'you'; mi is the indirect object 'me' ('if you understand me'). Note: the word se here means 'if' (conditional), not a form meaning 'you are'.
segments.inferno-24-g59: or = 'now'; fa is the imperative (second-person singular) of fare 'do/make' used to command; sì that follows fa is part of the idiom fa sì che meaning 'make it so that'; che introduces a subordinate clause; ti is the reflexive/indirect pronoun 'to you/for you'; vaglia (not listed among the words) is from valere and is a present subjunctive third-person form meaning 'may be of value/avail' — the full clause means 'now do so that it may be of use to you.'
segments.inferno-24-g1: This is a prepositional temporal phrase: 'In quella parte del giovanetto anno' = 'In that part of the young year'. 'che' introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('when/that') referring forward to the time described. The clause that follows uses inverted and poetic order: subject 'l' sole' + verb 'tempra' and then a second clause joined by 'e' with 'le notti ... vanno'.
segments.inferno-24-g2: 'quando' starts another temporal clause ('when'). 'la brina in su la terra' places the frost physically 'on the earth' with the double prepositions 'in su' (poetic emphasis). 'assempra' is a transitive verb taking 'l’imagine' as direct object: it 'forms/assembles' the likeness.
segments.inferno-24-g3: Contrast introduced by 'ma' ('but'). 'poco dura' is present indicative: 'lasts little' (i.e., is short-lived). 'a la sua penna tempra' uses 'a' + noun phrase to express the domain or respect in which the whiteness lasts little; 'penna' here is the instrument or figurative 'plume/pen'.
segments.inferno-24-g4: 'lo villanello' is the subject (the little peasant). 'a cui' is a relative pronoun 'to whom' (dative sense) introducing 'la roba manca' where 'roba' is the thing lacking and 'manca' is present indicative 'is lacking' — a typical impersonal/experiential construction.
segments.inferno-24-g5: Simple present reflexive verb: 'si leva' = 'he gets up / rises'. 'si' marks the reflexive/mediopassive voice and 'leva' is 3rd person singular present.
segments.inferno-24-g6: Two short coordinated verbs: 'e guarda' — conjunction 'e' + present indicative 'guarda' (3rd sing.) meaning 'and he looks'. The subject continues to be the villanello.
segments.inferno-24-g7: Coordination continues: 'e vede' (and sees). 'la campagna biancheggiar tutta' uses an infinitive 'biancheggiar' (poetic form of 'biancheggiare') after the perception verb 'vede' to indicate what he sees — the countryside 'whitening' completely.
segments.inferno-24-g8: 'ond’' (short for 'onde') gives a consequence 'whereupon/therefore'. 'ei' is an archaic 3rd-person pronoun 'he'; 'si batte l'anca' is reflexive: 'he beats/strikes his hip' (an idiom for indignation or surprise). Word order is subject-pronoun + clitic + verb.
segments.inferno-24-g9: Simple intransitive verb phrase: 'ritorna in casa' = 'returns home'. 'ritorna' is present indicative 3rd-person singular and 'in casa' is the directional complement.
segments.inferno-24-g10: Coordinated adverbial phrase 'e qua e là' = 'and here and there'. 'si lagna' is reflexive present indicative meaning 'he laments/complains'. Poetic repetition 'qua e là' emphasizes aimless complaining.
segments.inferno-24-g11: Simile introduced by 'come' ('like'). '’l tapin' is a poor/wretched person; the relative clause 'che non sa che si faccia' uses 'non sa' (he does not know) + 'che si faccia' (what to do — subjunctive/infinitive flavor in poetry) to express helplessness.
segments.inferno-24-g12: 'poi' = 'then/afterwards'. 'ried e' (here 'riede') is a poetic verb meaning 'comes back/returns' (cf. 'ridare' family). It is again 3rd person singular present.
segments.inferno-24-g13: Coordinated clause: 'e la speranza ringavagna' — 'and hope recovers/returns to him'. 'ringavagna' is a present indicative meaning 'recovers' or 'regains', with 'la speranza' as subject.
segments.inferno-24-g14: 'veggendo' is a gerund/participle meaning 'seeing/beholding'. The construction 'veggendo ’l mondo aver cangiata faccia' uses an infinitival perfect 'aver cangiata' (to have changed) describing the state perceived; 'in poco d’ora' is a temporal phrase 'in a short hour'.
segments.inferno-24-g15: Final coordinated actions: 'e prende suo vincastro' ('and he takes his crook') + 'e fuor le pecorelle a pascer caccia' — 'and out (he) drives the little sheep to graze'. 'fuor' = outside/out; 'a pascer caccia' is a terse, idiomatic sequence where 'caccia' has the force of driving/chasing out to pasture.
segments.inferno-24-g16: 'Così mi fece sbigottir lo mastro' is a causative construction: 'he made me lose heart / dismayed me', where 'fece' is the past-tense causative of 'fare' and 'sbigottir' is an infinitive complement. 'quand’ io li vidi sì turbar la fronte' is a temporal clause: 'when I saw him so disturb his brow' — 'li' is an archaic object clitic for 'him'.
segments.inferno-24-g17: Short clause: 'e così tosto al mal giunse lo 'mpiastro' — 'and so soon to the evil came the plaster/mess'. 'tosto' is an adverb 'quickly/soon'; 'giunse' is preterite 'arrived' (3rd sing.). 'lo 'mpiastro' is a metaphorical noun (a botch/plaster) used as the thing that arrived.
segments.inferno-24-g18: Single-word causal particle 'ché' = 'for/because', linking to the explanation that follows.
segments.inferno-24-g19: 'come noi venimmo al guasto ponte' — 'as/we when we came to the broken/ruined bridge'. 'venimmo' is passato remoto (we came/reached) and 'guasto' qualifies 'ponte'.
segments.inferno-24-g20: 'lo duca a me si volse' uses 'si volse' (volgere, past tense) reflexively to mean 'turned (himself) to me'. 'con quel piglio dolce' is an ablative/instrumental expression 'with that gentle bearing/look'; 'ch’io vidi prima a piè del monte' is a relative clause 'which I had seen before at the foot of the mountain'.
segments.inferno-24-g21: 'Le braccia aperse' is an absolute/elliptical phrase: the participle 'aperse' (open) follows the noun 'le braccia' — a poetic way to say 'he opened his arms'. This is not a full clause by itself but a vivid stage direction.
segments.inferno-24-g22: 'dopo alcun consiglio eletto seco' = 'after some counsel chosen with himself' — 'dopo' + noun phrase. 'eletto' is a past participle here ('chosen'), 'seco' is an enclitic reflexive 'with himself'. 'riguardando prima ben la ruina' is a present participle clause 'carefully looking first at the ruin' modifying the action.
segments.inferno-24-g23: 'e diedemi di piglio' — coordinated verb 'and he gave me a grip/hold'. 'diedemi' is an archaic past form meaning 'gave me' (cf. 'mi diede'), and 'di piglio' = 'in/with a grasp' — literal physical action.
segments.inferno-24-g24: 'E come quei ch’adopera ed estima' is a simile introduced by 'come' ('as / like one who'). 'quei' = 'that one/one who'; 'ch’' is the relative 'who'; 'adopera ed estima' are two verbs describing such a person (works/uses and judges/values). The verbs are in the present to describe a habitual quality.
segments.inferno-24-g25: 'che sempre par che ’nnanzi si proveggia' continues the relative: 'who always seems to provide ahead'. 'par' is present 'seems', '’nnanzi' (innanzi) = 'before/ahead', and 'si proveggia' is a reflexive/impersonal present meaning 'provides for oneself / takes precautions' — a description of foresight.
segments.inferno-24-g26: Single adverb 'così' ties this simile to the action that follows: 'thus/so'. It connects the descriptive comparison to the concrete help given.
segments.inferno-24-g27: 'levando me sù ver’ la cima d’un ronchione' is a participial clause describing the action performed while giving help: 'levando' (raising) + object 'me' + direction 'sù ver' (toward) + 'la cima d’un ronchione' (the top of a little rock/ledge). Note poetic contraction 'ver’' = 'verso' (toward).
segments.inferno-24-g28: 'avvisava un’altra scheggia dicendo' uses imperfect 'avvisava' to mean 'he pointed out/noted/indicated' — an ongoing or repeated action in the past — followed by the direct discourse marker 'dicendo' ('saying'). 'un’altra scheggia' is the object pointed out.
segments.inferno-24-g29: Direct speech begins: 'Sovra quella poi t’aggrappa' — 'Sovra' = 'upon/above', 'quella' = 'that one', 'poi' = 'then/next', 't’ aggrappa' is the present imperative/indicative sense 'cling to/ seize' with clitic 't’' = 'to you'. The verb is second- or third-person form used as instruction.
segments.inferno-24-g30: 'ma tenta pria s’è tal ch’ella ti reggia' — 'but first try whether there is such that it will support you.' 'tenta' is imperative/indicative 'try/test'; 'pria' = 'first'; 's’è' is a contracted form (si + è) meaning 'if there is' or 'whether there is'; 'tal' = 'such'; 'ella' refers back to 'quella' (the shard) and 'ti reggia' uses subjunctive-style sense 'will hold/support you' (present subjunctive/indicative nuance in poetry).
segments.inferno-24-g96: Tra + noun phrase places the action 'among/within' that group; 'questa cruda e tristissima copia' is a feminine demonstrative + two adjectives (cruda, superlative tristissima) modifying 'copia' (here: 'throng'). 'corrëan' is an archaic/poetic imperfect of 'correre' (they ran/ran about) with the subject 'genti' (people) immediately following in inverted word order for poetic effect; 'nude e spaventate' are past-participle adjectives (naked, frightened) agreeing with 'genti'.
segments.inferno-24-g97: 'sanza' = 'without' introduces a negative circumstance; the verb is the infinitive 'sperar' (to hope) used after 'sanza' rather than a finite verb. 'pertugio' means a small opening or escape-hole, and 'o elitropia' offers an alternative (heliotrope/precious stone believed to have protective powers). The colon marks explanation or amplification of the previous clause.
segments.inferno-24-g98: 'con' introduces the instrument: 'with snakes'. 'le man dietro avean legate' is an older word order: subject 'le man' (the hands) + adverb 'dietro' (behind) + auxiliary/verb 'avean' (imperfect of avere = they had) + past participle 'legate' (bound). So literally 'they had the hands bound behind (them)'.
segments.inferno-24-g99: 'quelle' (the snakes) is the subject; 'ficcavan' is the imperfect of 'ficcare' (to thrust/stick) describing repeated or ongoing action. 'per le ren' = 'through the loins' (preposition + plural body-part article). Word order 'per le ren la coda e ’l capo' lists the objects 'la coda e 'l capo' (the tail and the head) that the snakes thrust through the loins.
segments.inferno-24-g100: 'ed' = 'and' (e with euphonic d); 'eran' is the imperfect of 'essere' (they were). 'dinanzi aggroppate' places the state/result after the verb: 'dinanzi' (forward/in front) + adjective/participial form 'aggroppate' (hunched, bunched up) describing the posture of the people (or their bodies) after the snakes' action.
segments.inferno-24-g101: 'Ed ecco' is a compact exclamation 'and behold'; 'a un' introduces the indirect object/target 'to one' or 'at one'. 'ch’era da nostra proda' is a relative clause (che = who/that) with 'era' (imperf. of essere = he was) and 'da nostra proda' meaning 'on/by our bank/shore' — 'da' here indicates location 'at/near'.
segments.inferno-24-g102: The reflexive clitic 's’' attaches to the preterite 'avventò' (si avventò = 'sprang at' / 'attacked'); 'un serpente' is the subject. The relative clause 'che ’l trafisse' uses an imperfect subjunctive-like form 'trafisse' (here narrative past sense 'pierced') with the clitic 'l' = 'him/it' referring back to the man. 'là dove ’l collo a le spalle s’annoda' is a locative clause 'there where the neck to the shoulders knots itself' — 's’annoda' (si annoda) is reflexive/present form meaning 'is joined/ties' (poetic for 'where neck meets shoulders').
segments.inferno-24-g103: 'Né ... né' is a double negative 'neither ... nor'. The isolated letters 'O' and 'I' are treated as things being written; 'sì tosto mai' means 'so quickly/so soon ever' (poetic inversion), and 'si scrisse' is a reflexive/passive-like perfect 'was written' (si + scrisse), indicating that neither the letter O nor the letter I had been written so quickly — an idiomatic, elliptical poetic construction.
segments.inferno-24-g104: 'com’' = 'how/as' introduces the content clause describing the manner; 'el s’accese e arse' uses an archaic/reflexive-looking form 's’accese' (si accese = it ignited/was kindled) and 'arse' (past participle meaning 'burned/was consumed') to narrate the ignition and burning. The verbs are in a past narrative tense, compressed for poetic effect.
segments.inferno-24-g105: The coordinating 'e' links clauses. 'cener tutto' = 'all (became) ash' (cenere as noun). 'convenne che' introduces a clause of necessity/result ('it was necessary that...'); 'cascando divenisse' uses the gerund 'cascando' (falling) with 'divenisse' (imperfect/subjunctive of 'divenire' used here in a past narrative/result sense) to mean 'had to become by falling' — the construction expresses that, as it fell, it was obliged to turn to ash.
segments.inferno-24-g106: 'e poi che fu a terra' = 'and after that it was on the ground' ('fu' = simple past of 'essere'). 'sì distrutto' is an adverb + adjective 'so destroyed/ruined'. This clause sets the temporal background for the following action: once it lay thus destroyed on the ground.
segments.inferno-24-g107: 'la polver si raccolse' uses reflexive clitic 'si' with past 'raccolse' (gathered itself) — 'the dust gathered itself together'. 'per sé stessa' emphasizes 'by itself' or 'of its own accord'. 'e ’n quel medesmo ritornò di butto' means 'and in that very same (moment) it returned all at once' — 'di butto' is an idiomatic locution meaning 'suddenly/at once'; 'ritornò' is the past tense 'returned'.
segments.inferno-24-g108: 'Così' = 'thus/so' introduces the statement; 'per li gran savi' = 'by the great sages' (per + article + adjective + plural noun). 'si confessa' is a reflexive/impersonal passive meaning 'it is confessed/acknowledged' (si + present); 'che la fenice more e poi rinasce' is a subordinate clause: 'la fenice' (phoenix) + 'more' (here archaic spelling for 'muore' = dies) + 'e poi rinasce' (and then is reborn).
segments.inferno-24-g109: 'quando' introduces a temporal clause; 'al cinquecentesimo anno appressa' is a poetic way to say 'when it draws near the five-hundredth year' — 'al' = 'to/the', 'cinquecentesimo anno' = 'the five-hundredth year', 'appressa' = 'approaches/is near'.
segments.inferno-24-g110: 'erba né biado in sua vita non pasce' is a negative construction: 'erba' (grass) 'né' (nor) 'biado' (fodder/grain) 'in sua vita' (in its life) 'non pasce' — here 'non pasce' means 'does not feed on' (negative with non + verb). Word order places the negative objects before the verb for emphasis.
segments.inferno-24-g111: 'ma sol d’incenso lagrime e d’amomo' contrasts with 'non pasce' using 'ma' (but). 'sol' (solo) = only; 'd’' is the elided preposition 'di' ('of'/'from'). The list 'incenso, lagrime e d’amomo' names the rare substances it consumes: incense, tears, and amomum (a spice).
segments.inferno-24-g112: 'e nardo e mirra son l’ultime fasce' is a simple copular clause: 'and nard and myrrh are the final wrappings/bandages.' 'son' = present plural 'are', 'l’ultime fasce' = 'the last bandages/wrappings' — 'fasce' used metaphorically for funerary wrappings.
segments.inferno-24-g75: 'Io' is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I'. 'era vòlto' uses the imperfect of essere + past participle vòlto to describe a continuing state in the past ('was turned/facing'); 'in giù' is an adverbial phrase 'downward' placed after the participle.
segments.inferno-24-g76: 'ma' = but. 'li occhi vivi' is a noun phrase with the medieval plural article 'li' + 'occhi' (eyes) modified by 'vivi' ('living, active'); 'non poteano ire' uses the imperfect of potere to express inability in the past followed by the infinitive 'ire' (to go); 'al fondo' is the contraction a + il = 'to the bottom'; 'per lo scuro' uses 'per' in a causal sense = 'because of the darkness'.
segments.inferno-24-g77: 'per ch’io' is a short formula meaning 'whereupon I' or 'so I' (literally 'because that I'), used to introduce the speaker's immediate reply; the enclitic 'ch’' is the elided form of 'che'. The colon that follows signals direct speech.
segments.inferno-24-g78: A simple vocative: 'Maestro' addresses Virgil. In Italian poetry the vocative is simply the noun with a comma.
segments.inferno-24-g79: 'fa che' is an idiomatic peremptory construction: the imperative/present of 'fare' + 'che' introduces a subordinate clause requiring the subjunctive — here 'tu arrivi' is the present subjunctive ('that you reach/come to'). 'Da l’altro cinghio' means 'from/on the other girdle/belt/side' (da + l'); 'e dismontiam' is the 1st-person-plural hortative/indicative form (Dante spelling of dismontiamo) meaning 'let us descend/get down' and takes a direct object 'lo muro' (the wall).
segments.inferno-24-g80: 'ché' is an archaic/poetic form of 'perché' used here as 'for' or 'because', introducing the reason for the request that follows.
segments.inferno-24-g81: 'com’ i’ odo' = 'as I hear'; 'com’' is short for 'come' (as/just as). 'odo' and 'intendo' are present indicative verbs ('I hear' / 'I do not understand') used to report the speaker's immediate sensory state; 'quinci' means 'from here' or 'on this side'. Word order places the pronoun 'i’' (I) after 'com’' for emphasis.
segments.inferno-24-g82: 'così giù veggio' = 'thus/so I see down below'; 'veggio' is the first-person present of the (poetic) verb 'veggere/vedere' = 'I see'. 'e neente affiguro' = 'and I make out/figure nothing' — 'neente' is a strengthened negative pronoun 'nothing' and 'affiguro' is first-person present 'I can form/recognize/describe' (here 'make out').
segments.inferno-24-g83: Quoted phrase opening the reported speech: 'Altra risposta' = 'Another answer' (i.e., 'no other answer'). It functions as the beginning of the direct speech clause.
segments.inferno-24-g84: 'disse' is the passato remoto (simple past) of 'dire' = 'he said', third person singular; it is the narrator's tag for the quoted speech.
segments.inferno-24-g85: 'non ti rendo' literally 'I do not render/give you' — here 'rendo' means 'I give (you an answer)'; the object pronoun 'ti' marks the indirect object. 'se non' = 'unless' (literal 'if not'); 'lo far' places the object pronoun 'lo' before the infinitive 'far' (Dante's form of fare) meaning 'to do it'.
segments.inferno-24-g86: 'ché' = 'for/because'. 'la dimanda onesta' = 'the honourable/decent question'. 'si de’ seguir' is an impersonal/obligative construction (si deve → si de') meaning 'one ought to follow' or 'ought to be followed'; 'seguir' is the infinitive 'to follow'. 'con l’opera tacendo' = 'by (performing) the deed, while keeping silent' — the gerund 'tacendo' indicates accompanying silence.
segments.inferno-24-g87: 'Noi discendemmo' is passato remoto (we descended). 'il ponte da la testa' = 'the bridge from the head' (the 'head' of the bridge); 'dove s’aggiugne' is reflexive/impersonal si (s'aggiugne = si aggiunge) meaning 'where it is joined' or 'where it joins' and 'con l’ottava ripa' specifies 'to the eighth bank/shore'.
segments.inferno-24-g88: 'e poi mi fu la bolgia manifesta' — 'poi' = then; 'mi fu' is a dative-experiential construction ('it was to me'), with 'fu' passato remoto of essere: 'the bolgia became manifest/appeared to me'; the adjective 'manifesta' follows the noun.
segments.inferno-24-g89: 'e vidivi' is passato remoto first-person singular of 'vedere' (Dante spelling 'videre') = 'and I saw'; 'entro terribile stipa' = 'within a terrible thicket/tangle' (stipa = a dense mass), followed by 'di serpenti' specifying that the thicket is of snakes.
segments.inferno-24-g90: 'e di sì diversa mena che...' introduces a result clause with 'che' = 'that'. 'di sì diversa mena' = 'of so different a kind' (mena = kind/sort). The clause 'che la memoria il sangue ancor mi scipa' means 'that memory still takes/drains my blood' — a poetic way to say the remembrance still chills/drains him; 'scipa' is a rare verb here meaning to deprive/ruin or to take away.
segments.inferno-24-g91: 'Più non' + verb expresses 'no longer'. 'si vanti' is the pronominal form of 'vantare' (here present/subjunctive/optative sense in narrative: 'let Libya no longer boast' / 'Libya boasts no more'), and 'con sua rena' = 'with its sand' (i.e., 'boast of its sand').
segments.inferno-24-g92: 'ché se' = 'for if' or 'because if', introducing a hypothetical concession. 'chelidri' is a plural noun (water-snakes) continuing the catalogue of venomous creatures.
segments.inferno-24-g93: A continuation of the list of creatures: 'iaculi e faree' are plural nouns (types of snakes or poisonous things), followed by the verb 'produce' (third-person singular present) meaning 'it produces' — the subject understood is 'Libia'.
segments.inferno-24-g94: Further items in the list: 'e cencri con anfisibena' = 'and cenchri with amphisbaena' (both names of serpents/monstrous reptiles). 'con' = 'with' links the compounds.
segments.inferno-24-g95: Negated correlative 'né ... né ...' = 'neither ... nor ...' — 'né tante pestilenzie né sì ree mostrò già mai' means 'it never showed so many pestilences nor so wicked ones.' 'mostrò' is passato remoto 'showed'; 'con tutta l’Etïopia' and 'né con ciò che di sopra al Mar Rosso èe' are concessive/detached complements meaning 'not even with all Ethiopia nor with what is above the Red Sea.' 'èe' is an archaic/poetic form of 'è' (is).
segments.inferno-24-g113: 'E' is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and'. 'Qual è quel che cade' is a comparative/relative turn: 'qual' (which/what sort of) + copula 'è' + demonstrative 'quel' + relative 'che' introduces 'the one who falls'; word order is emphatic/poetic but literal: 'And which is that one who falls'.
segments.inferno-24-g114: 'e non sa como' = 'and does not know how'. 'sa' is present indicative of sapere used with an infinitive-like adverb (como) to mean 'know how'. 'como' is an archaic spelling of moderno 'come' meaning 'how'.
segments.inferno-24-g115: 'per forza di demon' = 'by the force of a demon'; 'per' = by/through, 'forza' = force/compulsion, 'di demon' = 'of a demon'. 'ch’ a terra il tira' is 'che a terra lo tira' contracted: relative 'that' + prepositional phrase 'to the earth' + object clitic 'il' (archaic form of 'lo' = him) + 'tira' present indicative 'pulls/drags'.
segments.inferno-24-g116: 'o d’altra oppilazion che lega l’omo' = 'or by some other oppression that binds the man'. 'd’' is elided 'di', 'oppilazion' (oppilazione) is an archaic noun meaning an oppression/obstruction, 'che' introduces the relative clause, 'lega' is present indicative 'binds', 'l’omo' is the article+noun 'the man' (archaic form of 'l'uomo').
segments.inferno-24-g117: 'quando si leva' = 'when he gets up'. 'si' is the reflexive/impersonal clitic and 'leva' (from levare) here means 'stands up/rises' (intransitive).
segments.inferno-24-g118: This relative clause continues: 'che ’ntorno si mira tutto smarrito de la grande angoscia ch’elli ha sofferta' = 'who, looking about himself, is entirely bewildered by the great anguish that he has suffered.' '’ntorno' is elided 'intorno' (around), 'si mira' is reflexive 'looks (at himself/around)', 'tutto' functions as an adverb 'completely', 'smarrito' is a past participle used adjectivally 'bewildered/astray', 'de' = archaic 'di', and 'ch’elli ha sofferta' is 'che egli ha sofferta' (that he has suffered) with 'ha' + participle.
segments.inferno-24-g119: 'e guardando sospira:' — 'and, looking around, he sighs.' 'Guardando' is a gerund expressing a simultaneous action; 'sospira' is present indicative 'he sighs' (here describing the sinner's reaction).
segments.inferno-24-g120: 'tal era ’l peccator levato poscia.' = 'such was the sinner raised afterwards.' 'Tal' = 'such', 'era' is the imperfect of 'essere' describing his state ('was'), '’l peccator' = 'the sinner', 'levato' is a past participle 'raised' (passive/resulting state), and 'poscia' is archaic for 'poi' = 'afterwards.' The structure expresses the sinner's condition when he was later raised.
segments.inferno-24-g121: 'Oh potenza di Dio' is a vocative exclamation: 'O power of God.' 'Oh' = O, 'potenza' = power/authority, 'di Dio' = 'of God.'
segments.inferno-24-g122: 'quant’ è severa' = 'how stern/strict it is.' 'quant’' is the contracted form of 'quanto' used exclamatively ('how'), 'è' = 'is', 'severa' = 'severe/strict.'
segments.inferno-24-g123: 'che cotai colpi per vendetta croscia!' — 'that such blows for vengeance rain down!' 'che' introduces the exclamative clause, 'cotai' = 'such', 'colpi' = 'blows/events', 'per vendetta' = 'as vengeance', and 'croscia' is an archaic verb meaning to fall/pour down (here 3rd person singular present), so 'croscia' = 'pour down/rain down.'
segments.inferno-24-g124: 'Lo duca il domandò poi chi ello era;' — 'Lo' is the article before 'duca' ('the guide'), and the following 'il' is the clitic object pronoun meaning 'him' (positioned before the verb): 'the guide asked him then who he was.' Word order puts the object pronoun before the verb, a normal older Italian pattern.
segments.inferno-24-g125: 'per ch’ei rispuose:' = 'whereupon he answered:' — 'per che' (contracted) introducing the consequence, 'ei' is archaic subject pronoun 'he', 'rispuose' is the passato remoto 'answered.'
segments.inferno-24-g126: '«Io piovvi di Toscana,' — 'Io' = I. 'Piovvi' is an archaic perfect of 'piovere' used here idiomatically to mean 'I fell' / 'I came down' (i.e. 'I came from' or 'I fell into'), so 'piovvi di Toscana' = 'I came/fell from Tuscany.'
segments.inferno-24-g127: 'poco tempo è,' = literally 'it is little time' — an idiom meaning 'it is not long' or 'recently'; 'è' is the copula used with the time expression.
segments.inferno-24-g128: 'in questa gola fiera.' — 'in' = in, 'questa gola' = 'this gorge/throat' (here geographical 'gorge'), 'fiera' = 'fierce/savage' (adjectival). The phrase names the place where he fell: 'into this savage gorge.'
segments.inferno-24-g129: 'Vita bestial mi piacque e non umana,' — 'Vita bestial' = 'a beastly life'; 'mi piacque' is passato remoto of piacere ('it pleased me' / 'I preferred'), with indirect object clitic 'mi'; 'e non umana' contrasts 'not human.'
segments.inferno-24-g130: 'sì come a mul ch’i’ fui;' — 'sì come' = 'just as'; 'a mul' = 'to/as a mule'; 'ch’i’ fui' is contracted 'che io fui' = 'that I was' (a self-description). Note archaic contraction 'ch’i’' for 'che io'.
segments.inferno-24-g131: 'son Vanni Fucci bestia,' — 'son' is the present of 'essere' used for 'I am' (archaic), 'Vanni Fucci' is the proper name, 'bestia' is a nominal predicate 'a beast.'
segments.inferno-24-g132: 'e Pistoia mi fu degna tana' — 'e' = and; 'Pistoia' (the city) is subject; 'mi fu' = 'was to me' (lit. 'was for me'); 'degna tana' = 'a worthy den/haunt' — idiomatically: 'Pistoia was a fitting lair for me.'

sentences.inferno-24-s13: He then raised me, showing me better supplied with breath than I had felt, and I said: "Go, for I am strong and bold."
sentences.inferno-24-s14: Up over the rock we took the way, which was rugged, narrow and difficult, and steeper by far than that before.
sentences.inferno-24-s15: He spoke so as not to seem feeble; then a voice came out of the other ditch, forming unseemly words.
sentences.inferno-24-s16: I do not know what he said, although I was already on the ridge above the arch that spans here; but the one who was speaking seemed about to go (was moved).
sentences.inferno-24-s5: There was no way to be cloaked, for we could scarcely — he light, and I pushed — manage to climb up from one buttock to the next.
sentences.inferno-24-s6: And if it were not that, more from that rim than from the other, the slope was short, I do not know about him, but I would indeed have been overcome.
sentences.inferno-24-s7: But because Malebolge as a whole leans toward the gate of the very lowest pit, the situation of each valley makes one side rise and the other fall; we nevertheless came at last to the point from which the last stone drops down.
sentences.inferno-24-s8: My breath had been so drained from my lungs when I got up that I could go no further; instead I sat down on the first landing.
sentences.inferno-24-s29: And I to the duke: 'Tell him not to be silent, and ask what fault here below struck him; for I saw him a man of blood and of anger.'
sentences.inferno-24-s30: And the sinner, who heard, did not pretend, but turned his mind and his face toward me and, painted with sorrowful shame, then said: “It grieves me more that you have found me in the misery in which you see me than when I was taken from the other life.”
sentences.inferno-24-s31: I cannot deny what you ask; I have been placed so far down below because I was a thief in the sacristy of the fine furnishings, and it had already been falsely attributed to another.
sentences.inferno-24-s32: But lest you fail to enjoy such a sight, whenever you are outside these dark places, open your ears to my announcement, and listen.
sentences.inferno-24-s33: Pistoia first wastes away because of the Neri; then Florence renews its people and ways.
sentences.inferno-24-s34: Mars draws vapour from the Val di Magra, which is wrapped in turbid clouds; and with an impetuous and bitter tempest he will be fought over the Picene Plain; whence he will suddenly break the fog, so that every White will be struck by it.
sentences.inferno-24-s35: And I have said it so that it should grieve you!
sentences.inferno-24-s9: “By now you must cast off such sloth,” the master said; “for, sitting on down, one does not come to fame, nor under a coverlet; without which whoever spends his life leaves such a trace of himself on earth as we were foam in the air and in the water.”
sentences.inferno-24-s10: And so get up; conquer distress with the spirit that conquers every battle, if his heavy body does not collapse.
sentences.inferno-24-s11: A longer ladder must be climbed; it is not enough to be separated from these people.
sentences.inferno-24-s12: If you understand me, now do so that it may avail you.
sentences.inferno-24-s1: At that part of the young year when the sun tempers its hair beneath Aquarius and the nights already move toward midday, when the hoarfrost on the earth fashions the likeness of its white sister, but that whiteness endures little in the temper of its plume, the little peasant who lacks possessions rises, looks, and sees the countryside all whitened; whereupon he smites his hip, returns home, and here and there laments, like the poor wretch who does not know what to do; then he returns, and hope recovers him, seeing the world has changed its face in the space of an hour, and he takes his crook and drives the little sheep out to graze.
sentences.inferno-24-s2: So the master dismayed me when I saw him so trouble his brow, and so quickly the ill (the bungled fix) came; for, when we reached the ruined bridge, the duke turned to me with that gentle look which I had seen before at the foot of the mountain.
sentences.inferno-24-s3: He opened his arms; after choosing some counsel with himself and first looking carefully at the ruin, he gave me a grip.
sentences.inferno-24-s4: And as one who both labors and judges, who always seems to provide ahead, so, lifting me up toward the summit of a little ledge, he marked another splinter and said, 'Cling then to that above; but first try whether there is someone who will hold you.'
sentences.inferno-24-s21: Among this harsh and most sorrowful throng ran naked, terrified people, without hoping for any escape-hole or heliotrope: with snakes they had their hands bound behind them; those (snakes) thrust the tail and the head through the loins, and (the people) were hunched forward.
sentences.inferno-24-s22: And behold, a snake sprang at one who was on our bank and pierced him where the neck joins the shoulders.
sentences.inferno-24-s23: Nor was O ever written so quickly, nor I, as how it caught fire and burned; and everything had to become ash as it fell; and after it lay on the ground so destroyed, the dust gathered itself together and in that very instant returned as one.
sentences.inferno-24-s24: Thus, it is acknowledged by the great sages that the phoenix dies and then is reborn when it approaches its five-hundredth year; in its life it feeds on neither grass nor grain, but only on incense, tears, and amomum, and nard and myrrh are its final wrappings.
sentences.inferno-24-s17: I was turned downward, but my living eyes could not reach the bottom because of the darkness; whereupon I said: “Master, see that you get to the other girdle and let us descend the wall; for, as I hear from here and do not understand, so I see down below and can make out nothing.”
sentences.inferno-24-s18: “Another answer,” he said, “I will give you none unless you do it; for a decent question ought to be followed by the deed in silence.”
sentences.inferno-24-s19: We descended the bridge from the head where it joins with the eighth bank, and then the bolgia became manifest to me: and I saw inside a terrible thicket of serpents, and of such diverse kinds that memory still robs my blood.
sentences.inferno-24-s20: Libya no longer boasts of its sand; for even if it produces chelids, iaculi and phareas, and cenchri with amphisbaena, it never yet showed as many pestilences nor ones so evil — not even with all Ethiopia, nor with what lies above the Red Sea.
sentences.inferno-24-s25: And like the one who falls and does not know how — whether by the force of a demon that drags him to the ground, or by some other oppression that binds a man — when he rises he looks round himself, completely bewildered by the great anguish he has suffered, and, looking about, sighs: such was the sinner raised afterwards.
sentences.inferno-24-s26: O power of God, how severe you are, that such blows for vengeance rain down!
sentences.inferno-24-s27: The guide then asked him who he was; whereupon he answered: "I fell from Tuscany not long ago, into this savage gorge."
sentences.inferno-24-s28: A bestial life pleased me, not a human one; just like the mule that I was. I am Vanni Fucci, a beast, and Pistoia was a fitting den for me.
